quality.rationale: 通过牛顿极限下相对加速度与测地偏离方程的系数对比，串联基础运动学与曲率张量定义，逻辑链条清晰完整

origin.question: Problem 12.12. By examining the relative acceleration of a family of test-particle trajectories in Newtonian gravity and comparing with the Newtonian limit of the equation of geodesic deviation, derive the correspondence$$R_{j 0 k 0} = \frac {\partial^{2} \Phi}{\partial x^{j} \partial x^{k}}$$between the Newtonian potential and the Riemann tensor. (A Newtonian test particle is acted on only by gravity; a test particle in a relativistic theory of gravity follows a geodesic.)
origin.solution: Solution 12.12. Let the Newtonian test-particle trajectories be$$\mathbf {x}^{\mathrm {j}} = \mathbf {x}^{\mathrm {j}} (t, n).$$Here $n$ is a parameter telling which trajectory we are considering. The vector$$\underline {{\mathbf {n}}} = \frac {\partial} {\partial \mathbf {n}} = \frac {\partial \mathbf {x}^{\mathbf {k}}} {\partial \mathbf {n}} \frac {\partial} {\partial \mathbf {x}^{\mathbf {k}}} = \mathbf {n}^{\mathbf {k}} \frac {\partial} {\partial \mathbf {x}^{\mathbf {k}}}$$is a connecting vector between neighboring trajectories. The relative acceleration of neighboring trajectories is$$\frac {\partial^{2} \mathbf {n}^{\mathrm {j}}} {\partial t^{2}} = \frac {\partial^{2}} {\partial t^{2}} \left(\frac {\partial \mathbf {x}^{\mathrm {j}}} {\partial \mathbf {n}}\right) = \frac {\partial} {\partial \mathbf {n}} \left(\frac {\partial^{2} \mathbf {x}^{\mathrm {j}}} {\partial t^{2}}\right) = \frac {\partial} {\partial \mathbf {n}} \left(- \frac {\partial \boldsymbol {\Phi}} {\partial \mathbf {x}^{\mathrm {j}}}\right) = - \mathbf {n}^{\mathrm {k}} \frac {\partial} {\partial \mathbf {x}^{\mathrm {k}}} \left(\frac {\partial \boldsymbol {\Phi}} {\partial \mathbf {x}^{\mathrm {j}}}\right) = - \mathbf {n}^{\mathrm {k}} \frac {\partial^{2} \boldsymbol {\Phi}} {\partial \mathbf {x}^{\mathrm {k}} \partial \mathbf {x}^{\mathrm {j}}},$$where we have used the equation of motion $\partial^2\mathbf{x}^{\mathrm{j}} / \partial t^2 = -\partial \Phi /\partial \mathbf{x}^{\mathrm{j}}$ . The equation of geodesic deviation is$$\frac {\mathrm {D}^{2} \mathfrak {n}^{a}} {\mathrm {d} \tau^{2}} = - \mathrm {R}_{\beta \gamma \delta}^{a} \mathfrak {n}^{\gamma} \mathrm {u}^{\beta} \mathrm {u}^{\delta} .$$In the Newtonian limit (velocities $\sim \epsilon$ , gravitational fields $\sim \epsilon^2$ )$$u^{0} = 1 + \mathcal {O} (\epsilon), \quad u^{j} = \mathcal {O} (\epsilon), \quad \Gamma_{\beta \gamma}^{\alpha} = \mathcal {O} (\epsilon^{2})$$and $\mathfrak{n}$ , which connects events of equal proper time, connects events of equal coordinate time to $\mathcal{O}(\epsilon)$ . Thus, $\frac{\mathrm{D}}{\mathrm{d}\tau} = \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{d}t} + \mathcal{O}(\epsilon)$ and so$$\frac {d^{2} n^{j}} {d t^{2}} = - R_{0 k 0}^{j} n^{k} + \mathcal {O} (\epsilon).$$The Riemann components are then$$R_{0 k 0}^{j} = \frac {\partial^{2} \Phi} {\partial x^{k} \partial x^{j}}$$in the Newtonian limit. Note that, unless the velocities involved approach the speed of light, the other components of $\mathbf{R}_{\beta \gamma \delta}^{\alpha}$ do not enter into the equation of relative motion of test particles.
